# Design: /full-review — Clustered Codebase Review Pipeline

Generated by /office-hours on 2026-04-06
Branch: kbitz/add-full-review-skill
Repo: kbitz/gstack-extend
Status: APPROVED
Mode: Builder

## Problem Statement

After 5-10 PRs land on main, cross-cutting patterns emerge that no single diff review catches. The existing `/review` skill (gstack) handles per-branch code review well. But accumulated drift — inconsistent error handling across modules, dead code from partial refactors, naming conventions that diverged in parallel PRs — only becomes visible when you look at the whole codebase.

The user currently runs a 22-line `/full-review` command from their global Claude config that dispatches 3 subagents (reviewer, hygiene, consistency-auditor) and synthesizes findings. It works, but the output is ephemeral (lost when the conversation ends), there's no triage step (you get a wall of findings), and nothing feeds into the documentation topology for tracking.

## What Makes This Cool

The pipeline: one command takes you from "messy codebase with accumulated drift" to "prioritized, triaged action items organized in your execution topology." The 3 agents provide genuinely different perspectives — implementation gaps, code waste, and pattern drift — and the clustered triage step groups findings by root cause so you're not clicking through 25 individual items.

The integration with /roadmap is the real multiplier. Approved findings land in TODOS.md tagged `[full-review]`, then /roadmap organizes them into Groups > Tracks > Tasks for execution. Weekly reviews compound: run /full-review, triage, run /roadmap, execute.

## Constraints

- Must follow gstack-extend skill conventions: single markdown file, YAML frontmatter, preamble update check, Conductor visibility rules
- Must integrate with existing documentation taxonomy: TODOS.md (inbox) → ROADMAP.md (execution topology)
- Agent definitions inlined in the skill file (self-contained, one file to maintain)
- No external dependencies beyond Claude Code's built-in Agent tool (available in all Claude Code environments including Conductor workspaces; if unavailable, fall back to sequential execution)
- Dedup against ROADMAP.md before writing to TODOS.md (don't re-flag known issues)
- All findings go under `## Unprocessed` in TODOS.md using the established source-tagged format; severity is encoded in the tag itself (`[full-review]`, `[full-review:important]`, `[full-review:minor]`) so /roadmap can parse it without format changes

## Premises

1. **The 3-agent split (reviewer, hygiene, consistency-auditor) is the right decomposition.** Each agent has a distinct lens: implementation gaps vs. code waste vs. pattern drift. Overlap is minimal (mainly naming conventions) and handled by dedup in synthesis.
2. **Whole-codebase scope is correct for weekly cadence.** Old issues should be flagged — the dedup step against ROADMAP.md prevents duplicating already-tracked work.
3. **TODOS.md is the right destination for approved findings.** All items go under `## Unprocessed` using the established source-tagged format with severity encoded in the tag (`[full-review]`). /roadmap drains `## Unprocessed` and can use the severity tag for prioritization.
4. **The skill belongs in gstack-extend.** Benefits from preamble/update system, ships with the project, gets the same polish as pair-review and roadmap.
5. **Agent definitions should be inlined in the skill file.** One file to maintain, self-contained, follows the pattern of the other skills.

## Cross-Model Perspective

Codex (GPT-5.4) provided an independent cold read. Key contributions adopted:

- **Reframe:** This is a "drift detector," not a "3-agent review tool." The identity is about detecting what main is becoming, not running generic code review.
- **Cluster triage:** Present findings grouped by root cause during triage, not per-finding. This is the UX insight that makes the skill usable at scale. Clustering is triage-time presentation only — TODOS.md gets flat items, /roadmap does the permanent organizing.
- **Build priority:** "If only one thing gets built, build the triage-plus-persistence layer. The prompts are not the bottleneck." We agreed — the pipeline matters more than prompt polish.
- **Deferred:** Codex suggested annotating findings with "why this emerged now" via git history correlation. Good idea, but adds significant complexity. Deferred to v2.

## Approaches Considered

### Approach A: Minimal Pipeline (Completeness: 7/10)
Single skill file, 3 agents, severity buckets, per-finding triage, TODOS.md integration. Ships fast but per-finding triage is tedious with 20+ findings.

### Approach B: Clustered Triage Pipeline (Completeness: 9/10) — CHOSEN
Everything in A, plus root-cause clustering in synthesis, cluster-level triage, severity tags on TODOS.md items, summary report in `.context/full-review/`. Better triage UX, carries priority downstream.

### Approach C: Full Drift Detector (Completeness: 10/10)
Everything in B, plus git-history-aware agent prompts, temporal annotations on findings, review history tracking. The full Codex vision, but trend tracking is unproven value per user feedback. Deferred.

## Recommended Approach

**Approach B: Clustered Triage Pipeline.** The clustered triage is the real UX win — it makes the skill usable when there are 20+ findings. The severity tagging ensures critical items get priority in TODOS.md. The summary report gives downstream skills (/roadmap, /ship) awareness of review state.

### Skill Architecture

**File:** `skills/full-review.md`

**Frontmatter:**
- name: full-review
- description: Weekly codebase review with 3 specialized agents, clustered triage, and TODOS.md integration
- allowed-tools: Bash, Read, Write, Edit, Glob, Grep, Agent

**Phases:**

0. **Active Session Guard** — Before any work, check for existing state:
   ```
   Glob pattern: .context/full-review/session.yaml
   ```
   If state exists, read it and present via AskUserQuestion:
   - Question: "You have an existing review session (started [date], [N] findings, [M] triaged). What would you like to do?"
   - Options: ["Resume triage where you left off", "Start a fresh review (archives the old one)"]
   If fresh, move old state: `mv .context/full-review .context/full-review-archived-$(date -u +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)`

1. **Preamble** — Standard gstack-extend update check (same pattern as pair-review, roadmap)

2. **Phase 1: Scoping & Agent Dispatch**

   **Scoping:** Before dispatching agents, identify focus areas to prevent context window exhaustion:
   ```bash
   git log --oneline -20 --diff-filter=M --name-only
   ```
   Extract the top 5 most-changed directories. Pass these as "hot areas" to each agent (agents should still review the full codebase but prioritize these areas).

   **Dispatch:** Make 3 Agent tool invocations in a single response block so they execute concurrently (if the runtime processes them sequentially, the skill still works — just takes longer; no user action needed):
   - **reviewer** (subagent_type: reviewer) — Implementation gaps, untested paths, logic holes. Prompted to focus on what would break in production, compare against specs/docs.
   - **hygiene** (subagent_type: hygiene) — Dead code, DRY violations, unused imports, overly complex functions, commented-out code. Grouped by file.
   - **consistency-auditor** (subagent_type: consistency-auditor) — Pattern drift, naming divergence, structural asymmetry, convention breaks. Compares recent code against established patterns.

   Each agent must return findings in this exact format (one per line):
   ```
   FILE: <path> | LINE: <number or range> | SEVERITY: <critical|important|minor> | DESCRIPTION: <what's wrong> | FIX: <one-sentence suggested fix>
   ```

   **Validation:** After each agent returns, check that output contains at least one `FILE:` line. If an agent returns prose instead of structured findings, extract what you can and annotate those findings as "unstructured — may need manual review."

   **Error handling:**
   - If an agent times out: proceed with the other 2 agents' findings. Note "reviewer/hygiene/consistency agent timed out" in the report.
   - If an agent returns empty: proceed with remaining agents. Note the gap.
   - If 2+ agents fail: present via AskUserQuestion: "Only [N] of 3 review agents completed. Proceed with partial results or retry?"
   - If all 3 fail: "All review agents failed. This usually means the codebase is too large for single-pass review. Try scoping to a specific directory."

   **State checkpoint:** Write raw agent outputs to `.context/full-review/raw-findings.md` immediately after dispatch completes. Write `session.yaml` with metadata (start time, agent status, branch, commit).

3. **Phase 2: Synthesis & Clustering**

   Merge all agent findings into a single list. Deduplicate: if two agents flag the same file+line or the same conceptual issue, keep the finding with the stronger justification and note which agents flagged it.

   Then cluster by root cause. The clustering heuristic:
   - Group findings that share the same file or directory AND the same issue type
   - Group findings that describe the same conceptual problem across different files — concretely: same issue type (e.g., "missing validation," "inconsistent naming," "error swallowing") even if files are in different directories
   - Singleton findings that don't cluster become their own 1-item cluster

   Target: 3-8 clusters. If you get more than 8, merge the smallest clusters. If fewer than 3, that's fine — small codebase, few issues.

   **Example 1 — Same directory, related issues:**
   ```
   [reviewer] FILE: bin/update-check | SEVERITY: important | DESCRIPTION: No error handling if git fetch fails
   [reviewer] FILE: bin/update-run | SEVERITY: important | DESCRIPTION: No error handling if git pull fails
   [consistency] FILE: bin/update-run | SEVERITY: important | DESCRIPTION: Uses different exit code pattern than bin/config
   ```
   → Cluster: "bin/ error handling gaps" (3 findings, severity: important)

   **Example 2 — Cross-directory conceptual grouping:**
   ```
   [consistency] FILE: bin/config | SEVERITY: minor | DESCRIPTION: Uses snake_case for function names
   [consistency] FILE: bin/update-check | SEVERITY: minor | DESCRIPTION: Uses camelCase for function names
   [consistency] FILE: skills/roadmap.md | SEVERITY: minor | DESCRIPTION: References camelCase helpers inconsistently
   ```
   → Cluster: "Naming convention drift" (3 findings, severity: minor)

   **Example 3 — Cross-directory missing validation:**
   ```
   [reviewer] FILE: bin/update-run | SEVERITY: critical | DESCRIPTION: No check that target directory exists before git pull
   [reviewer] FILE: setup | SEVERITY: important | DESCRIPTION: No check that source skill file exists before symlinking
   [reviewer] FILE: bin/config | SEVERITY: important | DESCRIPTION: No validation of config key names
   ```
   → Cluster: "Missing input validation across bin/ and setup" (3 findings, severity: critical)

   **Example 4 — Singleton:**
   ```
   [hygiene] FILE: skills/pair-review.md | SEVERITY: minor | DESCRIPTION: Commented-out debug line at Phase 2
   ```
   → Cluster: "Code cleanup" (1 finding, severity: minor)

   Each cluster gets: theme name, severity (highest of members), member count, member findings, one-line action summary.

   **State checkpoint:** Write clusters to `.context/full-review/clusters.md`.

4. **Phase 3: Dedup Against ROADMAP.md** — Read ROADMAP.md and docs/ROADMAP.md (whichever exists). For each cluster, check if the theme overlaps with an existing Track name or task description. Match heuristic: if the cluster theme shares 2+ significant keywords with a Track name or any of its task descriptions, it's a match. If matched, annotate "Already tracked in ROADMAP.md: [Group > Track reference]" and mark as pre-deduped.

5. **Phase 4: Triage** — Present clusters one at a time via AskUserQuestion (Conductor visibility rules apply). For each cluster:
   - Show: theme, severity, finding count, example findings (top 3 if cluster is large), whether it's already in ROADMAP.md
   - Options: ["Approve", "Reject", "Defer", "Done triaging — reject remaining"]
   - Pre-deduped clusters presented with recommendation: "Already tracked in ROADMAP.md — recommend Reject"
   - If user selects "Done triaging," all remaining clusters are marked Rejected and triage ends

   **State checkpoint:** After each triage decision, update `.context/full-review/session.yaml` with the decision. This enables resume if the session is interrupted.

6. **Phase 5: Persist** — Write approved findings to TODOS.md (root or docs/, whichever exists; if neither, create docs/TODOS.md):
   - Use the established source-tagged format: `- [full-review] <title> — <description>. Found on branch <branch> (<date>)`
   - All items go under `## Unprocessed` (same section /roadmap drains)
   - Critical items listed first within the section, then important, then minor
   - Write summary report to `.context/full-review/report.md`
   - Commit TODOS.md change: stage the file, commit with `"chore: add full-review findings to TODOS.md (<N> items)"`

7. **Phase 6: Handoff** — Present completion summary via AskUserQuestion:
   - Total findings, clusters, approved/rejected/deferred counts
   - Options vary by state:
     - If approved items > 0: ["Continue to /roadmap", "Done for now"]
     - If no items approved: ["Done"]
   - If no critical items found and branch has changes, also offer /ship

### Agent Prompt Refinements

**Reviewer** — Sharpened for weekly codebase review (not PR review):
- "You are reviewing the ENTIRE codebase, not a single PR. Look for cross-cutting issues that emerge from multiple changes accumulating."
- Focus on: implementation gaps between modules, untested interaction paths, assumptions that span files, error handling that doesn't compose
- Output: structured list with file, line, description, severity, suggested fix

**Hygiene** — Sharpened for accumulated waste:
- "Focus on waste that accumulated over time: functions that were partially refactored, imports that became unused after a module was rewritten, DRY violations where two PRs independently implemented similar logic."
- Do NOT flag style issues handled by linters
- Output: grouped by file, each with what/where/fix/severity

**Consistency-Auditor** — Sharpened for pattern drift:
- "Look for patterns that WERE consistent and have started to diverge. The most valuable findings are where 8 out of 10 modules do it one way and 2 recent modules do it differently."
- Focus on: error handling drift, naming divergence, structural asymmetry, import/dependency inconsistency
- Output: pattern inventory (3-5 established patterns), inconsistencies by severity, cross-module matrix

### State Management

All state in `.context/full-review/` (gitignored, conductor-visible). Written incrementally after each phase to enable resume:

- `session.yaml` — Session metadata: start time, branch, commit, agent status, triage progress
- `raw-findings.md` — Raw agent outputs (written after Phase 1)
- `clusters.md` — Synthesized clusters with triage decisions (written after Phase 2, updated during Phase 4)
- `report.md` — Final summary of review run (written after Phase 5)
- Future: `history.jsonl` — Review run log for trend tracking (v2)

**Resume flow:** On `/full-review resume`, read `session.yaml` to determine which phase was last completed. If agents finished but triage didn't, resume at Phase 4 with existing clusters. If triage finished but persist didn't, resume at Phase 5.

### Conductor Visibility Rules

Same pattern as pair-review:
- Every user-facing prompt uses AskUserQuestion
- Every AskUserQuestion includes an action receipt
- Never rely on intermediate text for important confirmations

### Error Handling

- **Agent timeout/failure:** Proceed with remaining agents' findings. Note the gap in the report. If 2+ agents fail, ask user whether to proceed with partial results or retry. If all 3 fail, suggest scoping to a specific directory.
- **Malformed agent output:** If an agent returns prose instead of the structured `FILE: | LINE: | SEVERITY: | DESCRIPTION: | FIX:` format, extract what you can and annotate those findings as "unstructured."
- **TODOS.md missing:** If no TODOS.md exists in root or docs/, create it with a `## Unprocessed` section.
- **ROADMAP.md missing:** Skip dedup phase silently. All clusters proceed to triage.
- **Session interrupted:** State is checkpointed after each phase. On resume, pick up from the last completed phase.
- **Empty results:** If all agents return 0 findings, congratulate the user: "Clean bill of health. No findings from any of the 3 review agents." Skip triage and persist phases.
- **Git not available:** If `git log` fails (not a git repo), skip the scoping step and let agents explore freely.

## Open Questions

1. **Severity calibration:** Should "critical" mean "would cause a bug in production" or "blocks the next phase of development"? For v1, use the production-impact definition.
2. **Agent tool availability:** The Agent tool is available in all standard Claude Code environments (CLI, desktop app, Conductor). If a future environment doesn't support it, the fallback is sequential execution (dispatch agents one at a time). This hasn't been an issue in practice.

## Success Criteria

- Running `/full-review` on gstack-extend itself produces actionable findings
- Triage completes in under 5 minutes for a typical run (< 30 findings)
- Approved items appear correctly tagged in TODOS.md
- /roadmap successfully picks up and organizes `[full-review]` tagged items
- No findings that are already tracked in ROADMAP.md make it to TODOS.md

## Next Steps

1. Write `skills/full-review.md` with frontmatter, preamble, all 6 phases, inlined agent prompts
2. Add `full-review` to `DEFAULT_SKILLS` array in `setup` script
3. Add routing rule to CLAUDE.md: "Code quality, codebase review, full review → invoke full-review"
4. Bump VERSION to 0.8.0 (new skill = minor bump)
5. Test by running /full-review on gstack-extend itself
6. Update docs (README, PROGRESS.md)

## What I noticed about how you think

- You said "I use this weekly not daily" — you have a clear rhythm for codebase maintenance vs. feature work. That discipline is why your project is at v0.7.0 with 21 clean PRs.
- When I proposed trend tracking, you said "I've never had it so hard for me to judge how useful it would be." That's intellectual honesty over feature hoarding. You don't build speculative features.
- You immediately connected /full-review → TODOS.md → /roadmap as a pipeline. You think in systems, not tools. Each skill is a stage in a larger workflow.
- When Codex suggested clustering, your first reaction was "isn't that redundant with /roadmap?" — you protect the boundaries of your existing tools rather than letting new features creep into their territory.
